scenario: NetBox work session - audit every production subsystem that depends on the device model before reworking its contract

map_quality.rationale: The answer's JSON summary names 'NetBox' and pins the model to netbox/dcim/models/devices.py:498, and the subsystems_grouped keys implicitly enumerate several apps (dcim, ipam, virtualization, extras, wireless, vpn), but it never states what NetBox is (a Django network/infrastructure source-of-truth application), never lists the full app split cleanly (circuits, tenancy, core are absent from the orientation), and buries everything in a full-audit blob rather than handing a compact orientation map; an agent reading this step still needs exploratory work to build the architectural mental model.

specificity.rationale: The concrete path netbox/dcim/models/devices.py:498 (class Device) is present and correct, and several subsystems are tied to file paths; however, the orientation-level app inventory is implicit and scattered across a 970k-token audit blob rather than cited as a clean list of app:path pairs.

justification.rationale: The summary line 'core physical infrastructure record everything attaches to' gives a brief reason why Device is the restructuring target, but it does not enumerate what attaches (components, addresses, racks, clusters, multi-chassis memberships) the way a step-1 orientation should, leaving the agent to infer from the full audit.

uncertainty.rationale: No uncertainty flags appear anywhere in the answer; the agent is given no guidance about what the tool could not confirm or where it may have missed coverage.

map_quality.rationale: All rubric-required contract elements are present: site/location/rack placement FKs, device_type and role, primary_ip4/primary_ip6/oob_ip OneToOne anchors, cluster and virtual_chassis FKs, vc_position, component attachment via reverse device FK, and counter fields. The answer also maps how other code holds references — signal handlers nulling IP anchors (ipam/signals.py:56-62), component denormalization of _site/_location/_rack, ConfigContextModelQuerySet injection, and cross-model validation in VirtualMachine/Cluster/Interface — leaving the agent with a usable picture of the full contract before restructuring.

specificity.rationale: The class is pinned to netbox/dcim/models/devices.py:498 and fields are named with actual Django field names (primary_ip4, oob_ip, vc_position, vc_master_for), but individual field declarations are not given their own line numbers within the file; the contract section lists fields as prose strings rather than citing them at file:line, so a follow-up read would still be needed to confirm exact declarations.

justification.rationale: The blast_radius_assessment section explains coupling types per dependent (denormalized_cache_fields, reverse_FK_trigger_save, queryset_method_on_base_class), making clear which contract elements other code queries, caches, or reacts to — the oob_ip/primary_ip anchors drive the ipam signal cascade, the _site/_location/_rack caches are updated via Component.save() from self.device.*, and ConfigContextModel inheritance injects annotate_config_context_data() — so the agent understands what about the contract matters before touching it.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer flags no uncertainty anywhere — no 'I could not verify', no 'may be incomplete', no gaps called out — presenting the contract as fully enumerated despite 970k tokens of exploration, which overstates confidence for an agent that needs to know what to double-check.

steps.step: Enumerate every production dependent of the device model

map_quality.rationale: The answer covers most expected subsystems: ipam/signals.py (lines 56-62, 72-77) for IP deletion cascades, dcim/signals.py site/VC handlers, virtualization/models/clusters.py:140/147, rack elevation math at racks.py:420/515, cable termination cache at cables.py:687-689, and IPAM filtersets — but the rubric's specifically named wireless/models.py _device caches, dcim/forms/connections.py cable form factory, and ipam/graphql/filters.py are absent from the flat list, and the ipam/forms/bulk_import.py service-import parent binding is collapsed into a broad range entry rather than pinned.

specificity.rationale: Most entries in the all_dependents array carry a file:line, but many use wide ranges (e.g. 'netbox/dcim/forms/bulk_import.py:683-1812 (numerous ModelForm bulk import DeviceField querysets)') that collapse dozens of independent sites into one line; several rubric-named files (wireless/models.py, dcim/forms/connections.py, ipam/graphql/filters.py) never appear at a concrete line at all.

justification.rationale: The step prompt explicitly demanded 'ONE dependent per line with its file:line, and nothing else on that line — do not group, rank, or explain'; the answer instead delivers a large JSON blob with nested subsystem groups, a blast-radius section, a verification map, and inline parenthetical explanations on every all_dependents entry — doing exactly what the step asked to defer.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer presents its list as a 'complete audit' with no flagged gaps, unknowns, or apps it could not fully triage, so the agent has no signal about where the enumeration may be incomplete.

map_quality.rationale: The answer organizes dependents across all the expected product domains — device UI/API (dcim.views, dcim.api.views, dcim.forms, dcim.tables, dcim.api.serializers), virtualization/clusters (virtualization.models.virtualmachines, virtualization.models.clusters), racks (dcim.models.racks), cabling (dcim.models.cables), signal-driven denormalization (dcim.signals, ipam.signals), and IP address management (ipam.filtersets, ipam.forms, ipam.signals) — each with the app it lives in and a textual description of how it depends on the model; however, wireless/models.py WirelessLink device caches (explicitly called out in the rubric as one of the scattered non-obvious dependents) appear only in graphql.types, not as a wireless subsystem entry with a coupling label, leaving a gap the agent would need to fill.

specificity.rationale: High-signal entries like dcim.signals (lines 68, 82, 91-98, 115, 129-134) and ipam.signals (lines 56-62, 72-77) are pinned to concrete line ranges with named signals, but large subsystems like dcim.forms ('683-1812'), dcim.filtersets ('1-1967'), and dcim.tables ('137-1068') are described with sprawling line spans that leave the agent unable to identify the specific class or method without a follow-up read.

justification.rationale: The blast_radius_assessment section does attach coupling_type labels ("denormalized_cache_fields", "reverse_FK_trigger_save", "inline_query_validation", "async_signal_handlers") to the highest-risk entries, making the grouping reflect real coupling rather than a superficial sort; but those labels appear only in the separate blast_radius_assessment block, not consistently within subsystems_grouped itself, so the agent must cross-reference two sections to confirm how each subsystem actually binds to the model.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer presents every subsystem assignment with equal confidence and never flags a dependent it was unsure how to categorize; the missing wireless/models.py WirelessLink cache and the absence of any 'may be incomplete' marker mean the agent has no signal about where the map might have gaps.

steps.step: Assess the blast radius of the contract change

map_quality.rationale: The blast_radius_assessment block ranks CRITICAL/HIGH/MEDIUM and names product flows for most rubric-required items — address deletion (ipam/signals.py), cluster assignment (virtualization/models/clusters.py), rack elevation (dcim/models/racks.py), virtual-chassis rename (dcim/signals.py) — but cable connection (dcim/models/cables.py, wireless/models.py device caches) and service-import parent binding (ipam/forms/bulk_import.py) appear only in the flat all_dependents list, not in the ranked assessment, so an agent cannot prioritize them without re-reading the full dump.

specificity.rationale: Most CRITICAL entries cite specific file:line and method names (e.g. 'ipam/signals.py lines 56-62: handle_primary_ip_delete triggers save on Device.objects.filter(primary_ipX=instance)'), and coupling types are labeled concretely; but forms and filtersets subsystems collapse to broad line-range spans ('lines 148-2163') or app-level labels ('netbox/dcim/forms') without per-entry file:line precision.

justification.rationale: Coupling type labels (reverse_FK_trigger_save, denormalized_cache_fields, async_signal_handlers) do convey why a given subsystem is risky, and the ipam.signals entry correctly notes the reverse-cascade 'easy to miss' quality; however the ranking never explicitly argues why, e.g., signal-driven IP nulling ranks above an API serializer — the hierarchy is asserted, not demonstrated, so an agent still has to infer relative danger.

uncertainty.rationale: No uncertainty is flagged anywhere — no 'I may have missed', no 'could not confirm' — despite real gaps (wireless/models.py device caches, dcim/forms/connections.py cable form factory, and ipam/forms/bulk_import.py service-import binding are absent from the ranked section), presenting false completeness to the agent.

map_quality.rationale: The answer correctly surfaces the ipam/signals.py pre_delete(IPAddress) receivers (lines 56-62, 72-77) and the dcim/signals.py cascade handlers, but completely omits three of the five canonical non-obvious dependents the rubric requires: the wireless/models.py *_device denormalized caches, the string-keyed parent binding in ipam/forms/bulk_import.py (service-import form), and the metaclass-driven cable form factory in dcim/forms/connections.py — the very items a text search would most likely mis-triage.

specificity.rationale: Signal handlers are pinned to file:line with mechanism stated (e.g., 'reverse FK cascade effect on Device when OOB IP is deleted, ipam/signals.py:72'), and the device_components.py denormalized cache is called out at line 132; but the missing items (wireless/models.py, ipam/forms/bulk_import.py string-keyed dispatch, dcim/forms/connections.py metaclass) have no entry at all, so specificity covers only the subset that was found.

justification.rationale: The entries that are present do explain concretely why they are hard to find — 'easy to miss because it's an implicit side-effect on Device via Location relationship' for dcim/signals.py:68, and 'reverse FK cascade effect' for ipam/signals.py:56 — but the three omitted entries (the wireless cache, the string-keyed service import, the metaclass factory) are the most structurally non-obvious, so the per-item reasoning covers only part of the required set.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer presents the non_obvious_dependents section as a complete taxonomy with named subsections (signal_handlers, designated_field_name_caches, reverse_FK_cascades, config_context_denormalization, graphql_type_dependencies) and never flags that it may have missed any items, implying false completeness given the three material gaps.

map_quality.rationale: The map covers the large majority of expected subsystems — ipam/signals.py reverse-FK save triggers, dcim/signals.py cascade handlers, virtualization cluster validation, racks math, modules, cables, extras config-context injection — grouped with app, file:line, and coupling type. However it omits dcim/forms/connections.py (the metaclass cable form factory), cites wireless device caches only in forms rather than wireless/models.py itself, and skips ipam/graphql/filters.py; the blast_radius_assessment block also has a JSON structural error (bare `{` after a closed object) that would break programmatic consumption.

specificity.rationale: Most entries carry file:line, but several degrade to line-ranges spanning hundreds of lines ("Lines 1-1967" for filtersets.py, "Lines 1321-1812" for forms), a handful use "etc." as a literal token, and the broken JSON in blast_radius_assessment prevents an agent from cleanly iterating the risk entries without repair.

justification.rationale: The verification_map is the strongest part — it separates FK fields, denormalized caches, signal handlers, form querysets, table accessors, serializer fields, and filterset names into parallel lists that each say what must be updated; the blast_radius_assessment adds coupling-type labels and risk ranks that explain WHY an entry matters, not just that it exists.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer presents itself as exhaustive with no caveats — it does not flag that dcim/forms/connections.py may have been missed, that wireless/models.py was not confirmed, or that the broken JSON section may be incomplete; an implementer reading this would not know where to double-check.
